title: Field Experience
text:
Below is an interactive map of all the field work that I have done over the last 4 years across the world. The majority of this work has been conducted with the LEAF lab but the points in Tahiti and Moorea are from my Wildlands studies experience this past summer.






Over the summers of 2024 and 2025 I was able to be a part of a field crew based out of Sequoia national park analyzing ancient forest response to fire in areas of low, medium, medium-high, and high severity burn. We measured water potential and hydraulic capacity (water availability within the plants, and amount of air within the xylem representing dead tissue.) and regeneration via germination count at plots from the beginning to the end of the summer.
In June of 2024 I was able to go with my graduate student mentor Mike Burnett to Tetiaroa to complete installation of the sapflow sensors we had been building for the year previous. Sapflow sensors are devices that when installed into a plant can track their hourly water uptake. On this trip we were insterested if coconut palms found in mass on the island due to an abandoned settle palm plantation used more of the islands limited water resources than the native Pisonia Grandis tree. A full report of my analysis can be seen in the projects section.
In the summer of 2025 prior to going to Sequoia national park I was able to return to French Polynesia to complete a 6 week field study program, completing various short experiements including pattern of sediment deposition and tropical fish counts at local beaches.

title: Biometry Final Project
text: This is the final project that I completed for EEMB 146 Biometry, showing code outputs, and a complete write up assessing differences in transpiration rate between two major species on Tetiaroa, an atoll in the South Pacific.

title: Talula Wilmot
text:
Bio
Hello! My name is Talula and I am from Alameda, CA. I moved to Santa Barbara in 2022 to complete my undergraduate degree in Environmental Studies B.S. which I will be wrapping up this winter. This site serves as a portfolio of my academic and professional skills gained throughout my time here at UCSB as well as a look into who I am outside of the classroom.


Currently
I am currently seeking employment opportunities in the conservation and education fields beginning in June 2026. I am currently working full-time as a lab technician with Lee Anderegg’s Landscape Ecophysiology and Function (LEAF) lab assisting with projects across the lab from predicting turgor pressure in Oaks during dry and wet years, to assessing ancient forest regeneration in low to high severity burn areas. I am also conducting a personal experiment analyzing how monocots, specifically palms, transpire water across their entire xylem surface.

title: About Me
text:
I will be completing my Bachelor’s degree in Environmental studies this March after 3 and half years at UCSB. Throughout my undergraduate experience I did take the opportunity to study abroad this past summer in French Polynesia where we stayed on the islands of Tahiti and Moor-ea for a total of 6 weeks completing various field studies and learning about the society islands rich history.
I have taken many formative classes during my time learning about a range of topics from the physics, chemistry, and biology, to environmental justice and what building sustainable communities looks like in the present time. Below are some specific classes that I have felt helped the most with professional my development as I begin my search for a full time position.

title: About Me
text:
Professional Development
While attending UCSB I have been fortunate enough to participate in a number of internships that have shaped my experience and the type work that I am looking for in the future.
I have spent the last 3 years working in the LEAF lab participating in a number of field experiences that are shown in greater depth in the Field Experiences section.
I have also worked with The Nature Conservancy offering field trips on Dangermond Preserve to students across the Lompoc school district teaching about the local Oakwoodland ecosystem. Most recently I have been working with UCSB’s Cheadle Center for Biodiversity and Ecological Restoration (CCBER) to give education walks to preschool students at both on campus child care centers.

 


Hobbies
When I’m not in nature with students I still love to be there with myself and my friends.
Most of my hobbies revolve around the great outdoors and include but are not limited to biking, climbing and

 

backpacking!
